items.stem: Which selectors require a comparison manifest supplied via `--state`? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: state:modified
result:fail
source_status:fresher
tag:nightly
config.materialized:incremental

items.rationale: state:, result:, and source_status: all compare against artifacts from a prior run, so they need --state. tag: and config: read the current project only.

items.stem: Is the statement a TRUE property of `dbt retry`?

options.text: It resumes the last invocation from the point of failure using run_results.json
It re-runs all upstream dependencies again from scratch
After a fully successful run it finishes as a no-op
In dbt Core you can override --select on the retry

items.rationale: retry reads run_results.json and resumes failed/skipped nodes without re-running successful upstreams; it is a no-op after success; in Core the prior selection is inherited and cannot be overridden.

items.stem: To build only the models that changed versus production, run: `dbt run --select "{{b1}}" --state {{b2}}`
items.rationale: state:modified selects new + changed nodes by comparing to the manifest passed with --state (a path to a directory of prior artifacts).

items.stem: Order the steps of a Slim CI run that builds only changed models.

items.text: Retrieve the production manifest to use as --state
Select state:modified to find changed nodes
Use --defer so unbuilt parents resolve to production
Build and test only the modified subset

items.rationale: Slim CI = compare against prod state (state:modified) and defer unbuilt parents to prod, so CI only builds/tests what changed.

items.stem: With `--defer --state <path>`, when does dbt resolve a `ref()` to the state (production) relation instead of the current target?

options.text: Only when the node is unselected AND does not exist in the current database (or --favor-state is set)
Always, for every ref in the project
Only for ephemeral models
Only when the node is part of the selected set

items.rationale: Defer resolves to state only if the node is not selected and is missing from the target db (or --favor-state). Ephemeral models are never deferred.

items.stem: Match each selector method to what it selects.
right: Nodes that are new or changed vs the state manifest
Tests that failed on the prior run
Sources whose data is fresher than prior state
Unit tests

key.state:modified: Nodes that are new or changed vs the state manifest
key.result:fail: Tests that failed on the prior run
key.source_status:fresher: Sources whose data is fresher than prior state

items.rationale: These are distinct node-selection methods; result: and source_status: also require --state.

items.stem: According to the 'Golden Rule of Materializations', what is the recommended progression?

options.text: Start with a view; make it a table when it's slow to query; make it incremental when the table is slow to build
Start with a table; downgrade to a view to save storage
Always build incrementally from the start
Start ephemeral; promote to a view when queried

items.rationale: Start simple: view -> table (when too slow to query) -> incremental (when too slow to build).

items.stem: How do `--empty` and `--sample` differ?

options.text: --empty limits refs/sources to zero rows; --sample builds against a time-based slice of real data
--empty skips tests; --sample skips models
Both build the full dataset but --sample is faster
--sample is only for Python models

items.rationale: --empty builds schema-only (zero rows) to validate dependencies; --sample includes a time-based sample of real data and needs event_time. --sample is ignored for Python models.

items.stem: A query reads from sample_data.public.customer, .orders, .nation, and .region (all in database `sample_data`, schema `public`). How many sources and tables are defined in sources.yml?

options.text: 1 source with 4 tables
1 source with 5 tables
4 sources with 4 tables
4 sources with 5 tables

items.rationale: A source maps to one database+schema combination. All four tables share sample_data.public, so it's 1 source with 4 tables.

items.stem: Order the steps for refactoring legacy SQL into dbt models.

items.text: Migrate the SQL 1:1 into dbt
Implement sources instead of raw table references
Choose a refactoring strategy (in-place vs alongside)
Implement CTE groupings and cosmetic cleanup
Split into staging/intermediate/marts layers
Audit outputs vs the legacy query

items.rationale: The guide's order: migrate 1:1, add sources, pick a strategy, CTE groupings, separate into layers, then audit with audit_helper.

items.stem: In dbt_project.yml, the default materialization is {{b1}}, and a config is distinguished from a folder name by the {{b2}} prefix.
items.rationale: Views are the default materialization; the + prefix marks a config (e.g. +materialized) vs a folder name. Configs cascade, most-specific wins.

items.stem: Match each property to defer or clone.
left: Lazily references prebuilt models (no new objects)
Eagerly copies a schema (real, persisted objects)
Objects usable in downstream BI tools
right: defer
clone
defer
clone
clone
items.rationale: Defer is lazy (no objects, CI); clone is eager zero-copy (real persisted objects usable in BI, CD).

citations.title: To defer or to clone, that is the question

items.stem: Is the statement TRUE about `dbt clone`?

options.text: It uses zero-copy cloning where available to copy a schema almost instantly
Cloned objects persist in the target schema after the operation
Cloned data always stays in sync with the source (no drift)
It can copy from multiple source schemas into one target at once

items.rationale: Clone zero-copies metadata to create persisted objects; it is point-in-time so data can drift, and it copies one source schema to one target.

citations.title: To defer or to clone, that is the question

items.subtopic: Implementing various testing steps in the workflow
items.stem: Per 'test smarter', which test failures should be set to ERROR severity (pipeline-failing)? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Customer-facing numbers
Numbers used for financial decisions
Executive-facing numbers
Nice-to-know checks with no immediate action

items.rationale: High-impact (customer-facing, financial, executive) failures should be errors; nice-to-knows should be warnings or removed.

citations.title: Test smarter not harder: add the right tests

items.subtopic: Using generic, singular, custom, custom generic, and unit tests
items.stem: You have a large incremental model and want a not_null test that does NOT scan the whole table every run. Which test config achieves this?

options.text: The `where` config to restrict the rows tested
The `limit` config to cap rows tested
`store_failures` to reuse previous results
`error_if` to stop at a minimum number of failures

items.rationale: A `where` clause on the test restricts the data scanned (e.g. to a recent date range), avoiding a full-table test each run.

items.subtopic: Implementing various testing steps in the workflow
items.stem: A source feeds a top-priority pipeline. How should you configure its source freshness check?

options.text: Run dbt source freshness in the job and set severity to error so stale data fails the job
Set severity to warn so it never fails the job
Skip freshness; only test primary keys
Only check freshness in development

items.rationale: For sources feeding high-priority categories, run dbt source freshness in job commands at error severity so the job fails on stale data.

citations.title: Test smarter: Where should tests go in your pipeline?

items.subtopic: Adding contracts to models to ensure the shape of models
items.stem: You added a check constraint and enabled the model contract, but `dbt run` fails with a parsing error about an invalid configuration for the created_at column. What resolves it while keeping the constraint?

options.text: Define the data_type for created_at in the YAML
Remove the check constraint from created_at
Change the materialization from table to view
Add a unique test to created_at

items.rationale: A contract requires each column's data_type; the parsing error is fixed by correcting the YAML (defining data_type), not by dropping the constraint or changing materialization.

items.subtopic: Defining constraints in YAML to enforce data integrity at the platform level
items.stem: Which statement about not_null and check constraints in dbt is correct?

options.text: They are enforced only after a model is built, so dbt treats them as definable but not part of the contract's build-time guarantee
They are enforced at parse time before any SQL runs
They are identical to dbt data tests
They are unsupported on all data platforms

items.rationale: Because many platforms enforce not_null/check only after rows are populated, dbt considers them definable-but-not-enforced at build time (unlike data_type, which is contract-checked preflight).

items.stem: In `dbt build`, a not_null test FAILs and the downstream model is SKIPped. How can you let downstream models proceed while still being warned?

options.text: Set warn_if/error_if thresholds so the failure escalates to a warning, which does not block downstream nodes
Add --fail-fast to the run
Set job_execution_timeout_seconds in profiles.yml
Run dbt run and dbt test separately; there is no other option

items.rationale: In dbt build, tests run before dependent models and a hard FAIL skips them. warn_if/error_if let you control when a test is a warning vs an error; a warning doesn't block downstream. --fail-fast does not unblock skipped nodes.

items.stem: What does selecting a raw table with `{{ source('jaffle_shop', 'orders') }}` accomplish in a dbt model? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: dbt compiles it to the fully-qualified table name (e.g. raw.jaffle_shop.orders)
It creates a dependency between the model and the source table in the DAG
It automatically materializes the source as a table in your schema
It lets dbt calculate and test source freshness for that table
It hard-codes database.schema.table so the reference can't be re-mapped

items.rationale: {{ source() }} compiles to the full database.schema.table and registers a model→source dependency (lineage). It does not materialize the source; freshness needs a separate freshness/loaded_at_field config; and using source() is what avoids hard-coded references, enabling re-mapping in one config file.

items.stem: Match each materialization to its defining behavior.
right: The default; rebuilt each run via create view as
Rebuilt each run via create table as; fast to query
Inserts or updates only new records since the last run
Builds no warehouse object; interpolated as a CTE into dependents

key.view: The default; rebuilt each run via create view as
key.table: Rebuilt each run via create table as; fast to query
key.incremental: Inserts or updates only new records since the last run
key.ephemeral: Builds no warehouse object; interpolated as a CTE into dependents

items.rationale: View is the default (create view as); table uses create table as and is fast to query; incremental only processes new records; ephemeral creates no object and is interpolated as a __dbt__cte__ CTE.

items.stem: Per the Golden Rule of Materializations: start with a view; when it's too slow to query, make it a {{b1}}; when that's too slow to {{b2}}, make it incremental.
items.rationale: The Golden Rule escalates view → table (when too slow to query) → incremental (when the table is too slow to build).

items.stem: Order the steps of the dbt process for refactoring legacy SQL into modular models.

items.text: Migrate the legacy SQL 1:1 into a .sql file under /models and dbt run
Implement sources with {{ source() }} instead of raw table references
Choose a refactoring strategy (alongside is recommended)
Implement the 4-part CTE groupings and cosmetic cleanup
Separate transformations into staging / intermediate / marts layers
Audit dbt output vs legacy SQL with the audit_helper package

items.rationale: The guide's six steps: migrate 1:1, add sources, choose a strategy (alongside), build CTE groupings, split into standardized layers, then audit with audit_helper.

items.subtopic: Logical flow of models & clean DAGs
items.stem: Is the statement a TRUE description of dbt graph-operator selection syntax?

options.text: `my_model+` selects my_model and all of its children (descendants)
`@my_model` selects my_model, its children, and the parents of those children
`+my_model` selects my_model and all of its children
The comma operator (`,`) forms a union of two selections

items.rationale: Trailing + selects descendants; @ adds the parents of a node's children; leading +my_model selects ancestors (not children); the comma is an intersection, while a space is the union.

items.stem: In `dbt_project.yml`, what is the purpose of the `+` prefix on a key such as `+materialized: table`?

options.text: It distinguishes a config from a folder/resource-path name, and the config cascades to that folder and its subfolders (most-specific scope wins)
It selects the model and all of its children, like the graph operator
It marks the config as required and fails the run if it is unset
It is only valid in model properties YAML files, not in dbt_project.yml

items.rationale: Inside dbt_project.yml the + prefix marks a key as a config (not a folder name); such folder-level configs cascade to every model in the directory and nested subdirectories, with the more specific scope overriding. The graph-operator + is unrelated, and underscores/dashes naming is a separate rule.

items.subtopic: Commands: build, run, test, docs, show, snapshot, seed
items.stem: Which single dbt command runs models, runs tests, executes snapshots, and loads seeds — all in DAG order — in one pass?

options.text: dbt run
dbt build
dbt test
dbt compile

items.rationale: `dbt build` runs models, tests tests, snapshots snapshots, and seeds seeds (and, from v1.11, builds functions) in DAG order. `dbt run` applies only to models and does not run tests, snapshots, or seeds.

items.stem: To validate that your models are semantically correct without expensive input reads, run a schema-only dry run by adding the {{b1}} flag, which limits refs and sources to {{b2}} rows.
items.rationale: The `--empty` flag limits refs and sources to zero rows; dbt still executes the model SQL against the warehouse to validate dependencies and that models build, but avoids expensive reads of input data.

items.stem: After listing a package in your packages.yml (or dependencies.yml) file, which command installs it, and into which directory does it install by default?

options.text: dbt deps — installs into the dbt_packages directory
dbt run — installs into the target directory
dbt seed — installs into the seeds directory
dbt clean — installs into the logs directory

items.rationale: You run `dbt deps` to install packages. They install into the `dbt_packages` directory (the default `packages-install-path`), which git ignores by default. `dbt deps` also pins packages via `package-lock.yml`.

items.stem: Every dbt Python model lives in a .py file and defines a function named model that takes two parameters: def model({{b1}}, {{b2}}). Inside it, read an upstream model into a DataFrame with dbt.{{b3}}("upstream_model_name"). The function must return a single DataFrame.
items.rationale: A dbt Python model defines `def model(dbt, session)` and must return a single DataFrame. Use `dbt.ref("...")` to read another model and `dbt.source("src","tbl")` to read a source.

items.subtopic: Providing access with the grants config
items.stem: A model inherits `+grants: select: ['user_a', 'user_b']` from dbt_project.yml. You want the model to grant select to user_a, user_b, AND user_c. Is this model-level config block correct for that goal?

options.text: {{ config(grants = {'+select': ['user_c']}) }} — uses the + prefix to ADD user_c to the inherited grantees
{{ config(grants = {'select': ['user_c']}) }} — clobbers and grants select to user_c only
{{ config(grants = {'select': []}) }} — revokes all grants instead of adding
Delete the +grants section so dbt stops managing grants

items.rationale: By default grants 'merge and clobber' — a more-specific config replaces the grantee list. Prefixing the privilege with `+` ('+select') ADDS the new grantees instead, so the model grants select to user_a, user_b, and user_c.

items.stem: Match each snapshot YAML config to its requirement or role.
right: Requires an updated_at column to detect changes
Requires check_cols (a column list or 'all') to detect changes
The primary key column(s) or expression identifying each record (always required)
Points the snapshot at its input, e.g. source('jaffle_shop','orders') or ref('my_model')

key.strategy: timestamp: Requires an updated_at column to detect changes
key.strategy: check: Requires check_cols (a column list or 'all') to detect changes
key.unique_key: The primary key column(s) or expression identifying each record (always required)
key.relation: Points the snapshot at its input, e.g. source('jaffle_shop','orders') or ref('my_model')

items.rationale: In YAML snapshots (v1.9+), `strategy` and `unique_key` are required; the `timestamp` strategy additionally requires `updated_at`, and the `check` strategy requires `check_cols` (a list or `all`). `relation` names the source/model the snapshot reads.

items.stem: Which statements about dbt's built-in incremental strategies are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The `append` strategy does not check for duplicates, so repeated source records produce duplicate rows.
The `merge` strategy without a `unique_key` behaves like `append`.
`insert_overwrite` replaces entire partitions wholesale rather than updating individual rows.
`delete+insert` is the recommended way to implement type-2 slowly changing dimensions.

items.rationale: `append` inserts without deduplicating; `merge` without a `unique_key` behaves like `append`; `insert_overwrite` replaces whole partitions. For SCD2 you should use dbt snapshots, not `delete+insert`.

items.stem: Order these config keys to complete a microbatch incremental model config block: materialized='incremental', then the strategy, then the time column it filters on, then the start-of-time boundary, then the batch granularity.

items.text: materialized='incremental'
incremental_strategy='microbatch'
event_time='session_start'
batch_size='day'

items.rationale: A microbatch model sets `materialized='incremental'` and `incremental_strategy='microbatch'`, then the required `event_time` column, `begin` (beginning of time for initial/full-refresh builds), and `batch_size` (hour/day/month/year). This matches the documented example order.

items.subtopic: Adding contracts to ensure model shape
items.stem: A contracted **incremental** model must set `on_schema_change` to `{{b1}}` or `fail`; using `{{b2}}` is disallowed because it removes deleted columns, which is a breaking change for contracted models.
items.rationale: Contracted incremental models require on_schema_change set to append_new_columns or fail. sync_all_columns also removes columns that disappeared from the query, and removing existing columns is a breaking change for contracted models, so it is disallowed.

items.subtopic: Defining constraints in YAML for data integrity
items.stem: Match each data platform to how it handles a column-level `check` constraint defined in a dbt model contract.
right: Supports and enforces check constraints
Does not support check; dbt skips it and raises a warning
Does not allow column checks at table creation time
Supports not_null and check constraints (via alter statements)

key.Snowflake: Does not support check; dbt skips it and raises a warning
key.Redshift: Does not allow column checks at table creation time
key.Databricks: Supports not_null and check constraints (via alter statements)

items.rationale: Postgres enforces all ANSI constraints plus check. Snowflake does not support check syntax — dbt skips it and warns. Redshift only enforces not_null and does not allow column checks at creation time. Databricks allows not_null and check constraints, applied via alter statements.

items.subtopic: Defining constraints in YAML for data integrity
items.stem: Is the statement a TRUE requirement or behavior of dbt `constraints`?

options.text: Constraints require the model to be materialized as table or incremental and to enforce a contract
Most analytical data platforms enforce only the not_null constraint
Constraints work on ephemeral and view models too
Setting warn_unenforced: False removes the constraint from the templated DDL entirely

items.rationale: Constraints require table/incremental materialization plus an enforced contract, and most platforms enforce only not_null. They never apply to view/ephemeral models. warn_unenforced: False only skips the warning — the constraint is still included in the DDL (warn_unsupported: False is what excludes it).

items.subtopic: Creating model versions & deprecating old ones
items.stem: All versions of a model keep the model's original name. `ref('dim_customers')` resolves to the `{{b1}}` version, while you pin version 2 with `ref('dim_customers', {{b2}})`. To sunset an old version with a migration window, set a `{{b3}}` (first-class since dbt Core 1.6).
items.rationale: An unpinned ref resolves to the latest version; ref('dim_customers', v=2) pins to a specific version. dbt Core 1.6 introduced first-class model deprecation via deprecation_date, giving consumers a migration window.

items.subtopic: Creating model versions & deprecating old ones
items.stem: Order the steps a model producer follows to manage change across an organization when introducing a breaking change to a versioned model.

items.text: Develop a new version of the model
Bump the latest_version to the new version as the canonical source of truth
Slate the old version for deprecation by setting a deprecation_date
Update downstream references to the new version
Remove the old version

items.rationale: The Model versions doc describes managing change as: develop a new version, bump the latest, slate the old version for deprecation, update downstream references, then remove the old version.

items.stem: Which model governance feature is NOT available in dbt Core (it requires a dbt Enterprise-tier plan)?

options.text: Model contracts
Model versions
Model access (public / private)
Project dependencies (cross-project ref)

items.rationale: All governance features are available in dbt Core and the dbt platform EXCEPT project dependencies (cross-project ref), which relies on the always-on metadata service and is available only on dbt Enterprise-tier plans.

items.stem: Match each step dbt performs when you run a command to the error type it raises if that step fails.
left: Initialize (is this a dbt project / can dbt connect to the warehouse)
Graph validation (compile dependencies into an acyclic graph)
right: Runtime Error
Compilation Error
Dependency Error
Database Error
items.rationale: Debug errors maps each pipeline step to an error type: Initialize -> Runtime Error, Parsing -> Compilation Error, Graph validation -> Dependency Error (e.g. 'Found a cycle:'), SQL execution -> Database Error. The error type tells you which step failed.

items.stem: When debugging, which directory contains plain `select` statements you can copy straight into a query editor to inspect a model's compiled output?

options.text: target/compiled
target/run
logs/dbt.log
target/manifest

items.rationale: Per Debug errors, target/compiled holds the select statements you can run in any query editor. target/run holds the SQL dbt executes to build models, and logs/dbt.log records all queries dbt runs.

items.stem: To reproduce one node's compiled SQL on demand, run `dbt compile --select "stg_orders"`. To read and validate your project code WITHOUT connecting to the data warehouse, use the `dbt {{b1}}` command instead. The compiled SQL is written to the {{b2}} directory.
items.rationale: The compile docs state that to read/validate project code without connecting to the warehouse you use dbt parse, and that compiled SQL files are written to the target/ directory.

items.stem: dbt run fails with: Compilation Error — 'Syntax error near line 5 ... mapping values are not allowed in this context'. Is the proposed cause the correct first thing to check?

options.text: Incorrect indentation in the .yml file at the cited line
A key that dbt does not recognize, such as 'hello', in the .yml file
A missing {% endmacro %} tag in a macro .sql file
A cycle in the model DAG that must be broken with {{ this }}

items.rationale: Per Debug errors, an 'invalid YAML' / 'mapping values are not allowed in this context' Compilation Error is usually an indentation problem at the cited line. An unrecognized key produces a different 'Additional properties are not allowed' error; missing {% endmacro %} is a Jinja error; a cycle is a Dependency Error.

items.subtopic: Developing, implementing & testing a fix before merge
items.stem: Order the steps for developing and testing a fix before merging it, following dbt best-practice workflows.

items.text: Create a git branch for the bug fix
Edit the model and run only what changed locally with the model selection syntax
Run a sandboxed slim-CI check: dbt build -s state:modified+ --defer --state path/to/prod/artifacts
Open a Pull Request and have the change reviewed before merging into main

items.rationale: Best practices for workflows: manage the project in git with branches, run only the model you're working on locally, gate the merge with a sandboxed slim-CI check comparing to prior production artifacts (state:modified+ --defer --state), and review every change in a Pull Request before merging into a production branch such as main.

items.stem: Flags (global configs) can be set in dbt_project.yml under a `flags:` dictionary, environment variables, and CLI options. In dbt Core v1.11+, the environment-variable prefix for engine flags is {{b1}} (for example, fail_fast becomes {{b1}}FAIL_FAST), whereas v1.10 and earlier used the {{b2}} prefix.
items.rationale: About flags (global configs): v1.10 and earlier use the DBT_ prefix, while v1.11+ uses the DBT_ENGINE_ prefix for engine flag environment variables.

items.stem: Which statements about behavior-change flags in dbt are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: They must be set in the `flags:` dictionary in dbt_project.yml.
They move through Introduction (disabled by default), Maturity (enabled by default), then Removal.
After opting out by setting a flag to false, you can stop the deprecation warnings using warn_error_options.silence.
dbt beginning to raise a new warning (rather than an error) is itself a behavior change.

items.rationale: Behavior changes doc: behavior-change flags must live in the flags: dictionary in dbt_project.yml; they progress Introduction -> Maturity -> Removal; opting out keeps deprecation warnings which can be silenced via warn_error_options.silence. A new warning is explicitly NOT a behavior change (only a new error is).

items.subtopic: Troubleshooting & managing failure points in the DAG
items.stem: A `dbt build` failed partway through the DAG. You fix the offending model and want to resume without rebuilding the models that already succeeded. Which command does this, and which artifact does it read?

options.text: `dbt retry`, which reads `run_results.json` to resume from the point of failure
`dbt run --full-refresh`, which reads `manifest.json`
`dbt clone`, which reads `run_results.json`
`dbt build --select state:modified`, which reads `catalog.json`

items.rationale: `dbt retry` reads `run_results.json` from the prior invocation and resumes from the failed/skipped nodes, leaving already-successful upstream nodes alone.

items.stem: `dbt clone` reads the source nodes from the directory given by the {{b1}} flag. By default it will not recreate pre-existing relations in the target; to force it to overwrite them you add the {{b2}} flag.
items.rationale: `dbt clone --state path/to/artifacts` selects the source; by default it skips pre-existing relations, and `--full-refresh` recreates them.

items.stem: Order the two CI commands so that modified incremental models build in incremental (not full-refresh) mode in a PR-specific schema.

items.text: dbt clone --select state:modified+,config.materialized:incremental,state:old
dbt build --select state:modified+

items.rationale: Cloning the pre-existing incremental models first seeds them into the PR schema so `is_incremental` is true; the subsequent `dbt build` then runs them in incremental mode.

citations.title: Clone incremental models as the first step of your CI job

items.stem: Is the statement a correct reason for including `state:old` in the CI clone selection `state:modified+,config.materialized:incremental,state:old`?

options.text: It excludes brand-new incremental models, which should full-refresh in CI just as they will in prod
It makes the selection more explicit so dbt won't try to clone models with no relation in the state manifest
It forces every cloned model to rebuild with --full-refresh
It is required or the clone command errors out on existing models

items.rationale: Brand-new incremental models don't exist in the prior state, so they should full-refresh; `state:old` excludes them explicitly. Without it you only get a benign 'No relation found in state manifest' message, not an error.

citations.title: Clone incremental models as the first step of your CI job

items.stem: Match each characteristic to the strategy it describes (defer or clone).
left: Explicit, run as its own command
Creates no database objects of its own
Point-in-time copy that can drift from source
Can dynamically mix multiple source schemas
right: defer
clone
Creates no objects (defer)
Drifts (clone)
Multiple sources (defer)
items.rationale: Defer is implicit (`--defer`), creates no objects, never drifts, and can mix multiple sources; clone is an explicit command producing persisted, point-in-time objects from one source to one target.

citations.title: To defer or to clone, that is the question

items.stem: What does `dbt clone` create when the data platform does NOT support zero-copy cloning of the source object?

options.text: A simple pointer view (`select * from` the source object)
A full physical copy of all the underlying data
Nothing — the command errors and aborts
An ephemeral CTE that is inlined at compile time

items.rationale: On platforms with zero-copy cloning (Snowflake, Databricks, BigQuery) dbt clones the table; otherwise it falls back to a pointer view that selects from the source object.

items.subtopic: Generic, singular, custom, custom generic & unit tests
items.stem: Which four data tests does dbt ship with **out of the box** as built-in generic tests? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: `unique`
`not_null`
`accepted_values`
`relationships`
`mutually_exclusive_ranges`
`not_constant`

items.rationale: dbt ships four built-in generic data tests: `unique`, `not_null`, `accepted_values`, and `relationships`. `mutually_exclusive_ranges` and `not_constant` are from the dbt_utils package, not built in.

citations.title: Add data tests to your DAG

items.subtopic: Generic, singular, custom, custom generic & unit tests
items.stem: A one-off assertion saved as a single `select` `.sql` file in your `tests/` directory is a {{b1}} data test, whereas a parameterized query defined in a `{% test %}` block and referenced by name in YAML is a {{b2}} data test.
items.rationale: Singular data tests are one-off `.sql` files in the `tests/` directory; generic data tests are parameterized `{% test %}` blocks referenced by name in `.yml` on models, columns, sources, seeds, or snapshots.

citations.title: Add data tests to your DAG

items.subtopic: Generic, singular, custom, custom generic & unit tests
items.stem: Is the statement a TRUE difference between **unit tests** and **data tests** in dbt?

options.text: Unit tests validate modeling logic against static inputs *before* the model is materialized, whereas data tests run *after* a model is built
A unit test result is always 0 (pass) or 1 (fail), while a data test reports the number of failing records
Unit test YAML definitions can live in the `tests/` directory alongside singular tests
dbt Labs recommends running unit tests in production to save compute

items.rationale: Unit tests run before materialization (TDD) and return a single 0/1 exit code per test case; data tests run after build and report failing-row counts. Unit test YAML must live under `model-paths`, not `tests/`, and unit tests should run in dev/CI, not production.

items.subtopic: Generic, singular, custom, custom generic & unit tests
items.stem: Every custom generic test should accept one or both standard arguments: `{{b1}}` (the resource it's defined on, always named this even for a source or seed) and `{{b2}}` (the column it's defined on, for column-level tests).
items.rationale: Generic tests accept the standard arguments `model` (always named `model`, even when the resource is a source, seed, or snapshot) and `column_name` (for column-level tests).

items.subtopic: Testing assumptions for models and sources
items.stem: Match each data test config key to what it does.
right: Sets whether a failing test is treated as `error` or `warn`
Saves failing rows to a table (in the `dbt_test__audit` schema) before counting
Filters the set of rows a test evaluates
Sets a threshold condition above which the test fails with error severity
Caps how many rows the test query returns

key.`severity`: Sets whether a failing test is treated as `error` or `warn`
key.`store_failures`: Saves failing rows to a table (in the `dbt_test__audit` schema) before counting
key.`where`: Filters the set of rows a test evaluates
key.`error_if`: Sets a threshold condition above which the test fails with error severity

items.rationale: `severity` is error|warn; `store_failures` persists failing rows to the `dbt_test__audit` schema; `where` filters evaluated rows; `error_if`/`warn_if` set thresholds. The extra option (`limit`) is the distractor.

items.subtopic: Testing assumptions for models and sources
items.stem: Order these config locations for a **specific instance of a generic data test** from **highest precedence to lowest** (most specific wins).

items.text: The test instance's `.yml` properties
The generic test's SQL `config()` block
`dbt_project.yml`

items.rationale: For a generic test instance, configs apply hierarchically by specificity: the instance's `.yml` properties override the generic SQL definition's `config()`, which overrides values set in `dbt_project.yml`.

items.subtopic: Implementing testing steps in the workflow
items.stem: Per the *test smarter, not harder* framework, which concerns should be **pipeline-failing** (set to `severity: error`)? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Customer-facing data
Data used for financial decisions
Executive-facing data
Any nice-to-know concern you might act on someday

items.rationale: High-impact concerns — customer-facing, used for financial decisions, or executive-facing — get `error` severity. Everything else is a nice-to-know: set to `warn` or remove it.

citations.title: Test smarter not harder: add the right tests to your dbt project

items.subtopic: Implementing testing steps in the workflow
items.stem: Match each pipeline layer to the testing guidance from *where should tests go in your pipeline?*
right: Test fixable-at-the-source issues and run `dbt source freshness` (severity `error` for top-priority sources)
Focus on business-anomaly detection; don't re-test your own cleanup
Add unit tests for complex transformation logic plus anomaly tests on net-new columns
Only ever add `not_null` tests, nowhere else

key.Sources: Test fixable-at-the-source issues and run `dbt source freshness` (severity `error` for top-priority sources)
key.Staging: Focus on business-anomaly detection; don't re-test your own cleanup
key.Marts: Add unit tests for complex transformation logic plus anomaly tests on net-new columns

items.rationale: Sources get fixable-at-source tests + `dbt source freshness` (error for critical sources); staging focuses on business anomalies without re-testing cleanup; marts add unit tests for complex logic and anomaly tests on new calculated fields. The last option is a distractor.

citations.title: Test smarter not harder: Where should tests go in your pipeline?

items.stem: Which properties are **required** when declaring an exposure in YAML? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: name
type
owner
depends_on
maturity

items.rationale: name (unique, snake_case), type (one of dashboard/notebook/analysis/ml/application), and owner (name or email) are required. depends_on is 'expected' but not required, and maturity is optional.

items.stem:
To run an exposure named `weekly_jaffle_report` together with all of the models that feed it, use:

`dbt run -s {{b1}}weekly_jaffle_report`
items.rationale: The exposure: selection method targets an exposure; the leading + operator pulls in its upstream parents (the resources that feed it), e.g. dbt run -s +exposure:weekly_jaffle_report.

items.stem: Match each exposure property to its correct description.
right: One of dashboard, notebook, analysis, ml, application
One of high, medium, low — confidence/stability of the exposure
Requires name or email; supports name and email only
Human-friendly name that may contain spaces and capital letters

key.type: One of dashboard, notebook, analysis, ml, application
key.maturity: One of high, medium, low — confidence/stability of the exposure
key.owner: Requires name or email; supports name and email only
key.label: Human-friendly name that may contain spaces and capital letters

items.rationale: type categorizes the exposure in docs; maturity (high/medium/low) signals confidence; owner needs name or email; label provides a display name since name itself must be snake_case.

items.stem: When `dbt source freshness` completes, it writes a JSON file to the path {{b1}} by default. To send it elsewhere, pass the {{b2}} flag.
items.rationale: dbt source freshness saves results to target/sources.json; the destination is overridden with -o / --output (e.g. --output target/source_freshness.json).

items.stem: Is the statement a TRUE property of source freshness in dbt Core 1.11?

options.text: If neither warn_after nor error_after is provided in a freshness block, dbt will not calculate freshness for those tables.
Setting `freshness: null` on a table excludes it from freshness calculations.
`dbt build` automatically runs source freshness checks before building models.
A table-level freshness block is merged with, rather than overriding, the source-level block.

items.rationale: Freshness needs at least one of warn_after/error_after or it is skipped; freshness: null excludes a table. dbt build does NOT run freshness, and table-level blocks OVERRIDE (not merge with) source-level blocks.

items.stem: Order the steps to monitor source staleness so that a downstream run can target only models whose sources have new data.

items.text: Add a freshness block with warn_after/error_after and a loaded_at_field to the source config
Run `dbt source freshness`, which queries the sources and writes target/sources.json
Pass the prior sources.json via --state on a later invocation
Select only refreshed models with the source_status:fresher method

items.rationale: You first declare freshness thresholds and the loaded_at_field, then run dbt source freshness to produce target/sources.json, then supply that artifact via --state, enabling source_status:fresher to pick only models whose sources have newer data.
